definitions: (semantics, philosophy) The relationship of being a constituent part or member of something; a system of meronyms.
examples: But whereas hyponymy is a member–class relation, reflecting a taxonomy or conceptual hierarchy, meronymy is a part–whole relation, reflecting the existence of complex structures in concrete reality.

Possession, like meronymy, is described in English (and equivalently in other languages) with the verb to have (A millionaire has money) and the line between possession and part-having is fuzzy at best. […] Priss (1998) suggests that meronymy might be formalized as an attribution relation, such that has-a-handle-for-a-part would be an attribute of hammer and cup. Thus, the case for separating attribution and possession from meronymy is not strong.

This relationship of meronymy is controversial for various reasons. First, there are several types of meronymy, such as functional meronymy, where one concept is a functional part of another (e.g. FINGER-HAND) or more general part-whole relations, where the part and the whole exist as a continuous entity (e.g. FLAME-FIRE). Secondly, there are diverging opinions as to whether meronymy should be treated as a semantic primitive in the sense of s[yn]onymy, antonymy, and hyponymy.

THE KEY CONCEPT IS “ metonymy.” This is the act of a word or phrase associated with a concept coming to represent the entirety of said concept. Generally, this means an extension of the original meaning. For example, “city hall” no longer means just the structure, but also the lawmakers who inhabit that structure. In the same way, “dish” started as the physical plate a meal is served on, before people started using it to represent the meal itself.

" Mu," said Kelly Dahl. On one level mu means only yes, but on a deeper level of Zen it was often used by the master when the acolyte asked a stupid, unanswerable or wrongheaded question such as "Does a dog have the Buddha-nature?" The Master would answer only, " Mu," meaning— I say "yes" but mean "no," but the actual answer is: Unask the question.

Mu means "no thing." Like "Quality" it points outside the process of dualistic discrimination. Mu simply says, "No class; not one, not zero, not yes, not no." […] It's a great mistake, a kind of dishonesty, to sweep nature's mu answers under the carpet.

[p. 233] Achilles: Oh, but ‘ MU ’ is Jōshū’s answer. By saying ‘ MU ’, Jōshū let the other monk know that only by not asking such questions can one know the answer to them. Tortoise: Jōshū “unasked” the question. [p. 312] Achilles: […] And the answer of “ MU ” here rejects the premises of the question, which are that one or the other must be chosen.

The Fifth Patriarch's utterance You say mu [Buddha-nature] because Buddha-nature is emptiness articulates clearly and distinctly the truth that emptiness is not "no". In uttering Buddha-nature-emptiness one does not say "half a pound." One does not say "eight ounces." One says " mu."

definitions: (chiefly Scotland) A piece of the forward end of the plow-beam by which the traces are attached.
The protruding part of an animal's head which includes the nose, mouth and jaws.
(firearms) The mouth or the end for entrance or discharge of a gun, pistol etc., that the bullet emerges from.
A device used to prevent an animal from biting or eating, which is worn on its snout.
(slang, derogatory, by extension) A person's mouth.
examples: The yellow fog that rubs its back upon the window-panes, / The yellow smoke that rubs its muzzle on the window-panes, […]

definitions: To bind or confine an animal's mouth by putting a muzzle, as to prevent it from eating or biting.

To bring the muzzle or mouth near.
examples: The Bear comes directly up to him, Muzzles and Smells to him.

Thou shalt not muzzle the ox when he treadeth out the corn.

definitions: Of a gun, having ammunition loaded from the front of the barrel where it will exit.

definitions: Bewildered; dazed.
Tipsy; drunk; involving drunkenness.

To the muzzy drumming of beer in his veins he gazed long and owlishly at the sombre cottage[.]

Why laſt night, as Colonel Kill'em, Sir William Weezy, Lord Frederick Foretop, and I were careleſsly ſliding the Ranelagh round, picking our teeth, after a damn'd muzzy dinner at Boodle's, who ſhould trip by but an abbeſs, well known about town, with a ſmart little nun in her ſuite.
The front bar was plying trade too, by customers that kept oozing in through the back door. The fat publican served whoever came, muzzy with the succession of pots that stodged away his day.

definitions: (computing) Abbreviation of move.
(medicine, pathology) Muscular pain or tenderness.
examples: Southern physicians were prone to calling all fevers, malaises, myalgias, and headaches "malaria" and to treating them with quinine.

definitions: Pertaining to myalgia.
(medicine) Abnormal weakness of the muscles; but especially myasthenia gravis.
A person who has myasthenia.
Of, pertaining to, or suffering from myasthenia.
plural of mycelium
The vegetative part of any fungus, consisting of a mass of branching, threadlike hyphae, often underground.
plural of mycobacterium

A person who studies, professes or practices mycology.
examples: ‘Iʼm not a mycologist but I believe you only get this formation when the fungus is forming reproductive organs.’

definitions: The study of fungi.
(biology) Any organism that eats fungus.
A person who eats fungi.
An organism or animal that feeds on fungi.
(biology) The eating of fungus.
Any infectious bacterium of the genus Mycoplasma, often specifically Mycoplasma pneumoniae
examples: Officials are investigating whether the illness was caused by mycoplasma, a common bacteria known as walking pneumonia.

This evolutionary trajectory assumes the simplicity to complexity route of biogenesis, a point of view that is not universally accepted.

definitions: Of or pertaining to mycoplasma.
(pathology) An infection caused by a fungus.
(biochemistry) Any substance, produced by a mold or fungus, that is injurious to vertebrates upon ingestion, inhalation or skin contact.
examples: They damaged his nervous system with a wartime Russian mycotoxin.

definitions: (medicine) The condition of having abnormally large and dilated pupils due to illness or narcotics, particularly stimulants such as methamphetamine, cocaine, etc.
(pharmacology) A drug that causes mydriasis.
(biology, medicine) Pertaining to or causing mydriasis (the dilation of the pupil of the eye).
(neuroanatomy) The portion of the brain composed of the medulla oblongata.
(neuroanatomy) A white, fatty material, composed of lipids and lipoproteins, that surrounds the axons of nerves.
examples: This gene controls the production of an insulating sleeve that wraps around neurons. Known as myelin, it is crucial for long-range communication in the brain.

As the result of prolonged action of water upon myelin, bird's-nests-cells are also said to be produced, and we are certain we have seen, as the result of such prolonged action of water, the appearances thus compared.

definitions: (anatomy) Of nerves, having a coating of myelin.
Relating to myelin.
myelination
Inflammation of the spinal cord or of bone marrow.
(biology) An immature cell of bone marrow that develops into a myelocyte.
Of, pertaining to or containing myeloblasts.
A large cell, found in bone marrow, that becomes a granulocyte when mature.
A cell associated with the myelin of the brain and spinal cord.
examples: Coordinate terms: promyelocyte, metamyelocyte

definitions: Of or pertaining to myelocytes
collagen deposition by fibroblasts in the bone marrow, whether primary or secondary to other causes such as medication.
A radiographic image produced by myelography.
(medicine) A type of radiographic examination that uses a contrast medium to detect pathology of the spinal cord, including the location of a spinal cord injury, cysts, and tumors.
Such a stem cell
(less commonly used) Of or pertaining to the spinal cord.
(hematology) Of or pertaining to bone marrow.
(pathology, oncology) A malignant tumour arising from cells of the bone marrow, specifically plasma cells.
examples: While Powell was fully vaccinated, he was also battling multiple myeloma, a cancer that suppresses the immune system, making him more susceptible to the virus and less likely to mount an immune response after being vaccinated.

definitions: (pathology) A form of spina bifida characterized by protrusion of the spinal meninges.
(anatomy) Of or pertaining to the myenteron.
(medicine) The infestation of a living vertebrate by maggots.
A polyester film.
examples: In the far infrared a self-supporting layer, such as a sheet of mylar stretched over a plane circular surface, is widely used.

definitions: (zoology) Any of the extinct genus † Mylodon of giant ground sloths of Patagonia.

One of the South and East Asian birds of the starling family Sturnidae.

definitions: (anatomy, cardiology) The muscular substance of the heart; the middle of the three layers forming the outer wall of the human heart.
(medicine) The brief, involuntary twitching of a muscle or group of muscles.
(anatomy) Any of the cylindrical organelles, found within muscle cells, that are the contractile unit of muscles.

(anatomy) A muscle-like structure.
(anatomy) Composed of or resembling muscle.

examples: He greets me as if I'd been waiting all my life to learn the secrets of Bovine Myology and Muscle Profiling.

Given this, there is an urgent need for detailed compared behavioural and anatomical studies of dasyurids, especially focused on osteology, myology and arthrology.

definitions: (pathology) A tumor composed of muscle tissue, usually benign and commonly occurring in the uterus and the esophagus.
(medicine) Inflammation of the myometrium.
(anatomy) The smooth muscle forming the wall of the uterus.
necrosis of muscle tissue
neuromuscular; relating to the muscles and nerves
Pertaining to myopathy
(medicine) Any of several diseases of muscle that are not caused by nerve disorders
One who has myopia.
(pathology) A disorder of the vision where distant objects appear blurred because the eye focuses their images in front of the retina instead of on it.
(figurative) A lack of imagination, discernment or long-range perspective in thinking or planning.
examples: The modern rise in myopia mirrored a trend for children in many countries to spend more time engaged in reading, studying or — more recently — glued to computer and smartphone screens.

definitions: A short-sighted individual.
examples: The offbeat little girls of “Playdate,” whose mothers stumble through parenthood, are not the first characters to feel like cultural descendants of Salinger’s children, those savants, myopics, guileless nose pickers and practicing belchers who seem to glow on the page, highlighting the shallowness of the adults.

definitions: (figurative) Narrow-minded.
Near-sighted; unable to see distant objects unaided.
(figurative) Shortsighted; improvident.
examples: Scientific advances can draw us outside of our myopic comfort zone.
A stronger prescription for myopic night drivers is often needed.
Corrective lenses compensate for the excessive positive diopters of the myopic eye.
His postwar roles, full of myopic obsession, stalkerish derangement, and a desire for vengeance, don't seem like ground Hanks is willing to cover.

The real problem, he insists, is the myopic mentality that has failed to address climate change to date. The rich world’s indifference to the despoliation of the environment in pursuit of short-term economic gain is rooted in a wider problem.

definitions: A malignant muscle tumor.

Inflammation of the muscles.
Any plant of the genus Myosotis, the forget-me-nots.

How would he, then, describe Tilly's manner and appearance at breakfast? Peregrine shrugged and said it was much as usual; she was gay and very pretty in her dirndl skirt and blue and yellow silk apron embroidered with myosotis.

definitions: A mouse-eared bat.

examples: If a hyperdeviation is present in an eye treated with such a myotomy of a horizontal rectus, the proximal myotomy should begin above to shift the insertion downward and make the horizontal rectus more effective as a depressor.

definitions: A symptom of several muscular disorders characterized by the slow relaxation of the muscles after voluntary contraction or electrical stimulation.
Of or pertaining to myotonia.
(formal) Ten thousand; 10,000 [from 16th c.]

examples: Earth hosts a myriad of animals.

definitions: (modifying a plural noun) Great in number; innumerable, multitudinous [from 18th c.]
(modifying a singular noun) Multifaceted, having innumerable elements [from 18th c.]

one night he would be singing at the barred window and yelling down out of the soft myriad darkness of a May night; the next night he would be gone [...].

pertaining to the anteater
feeding on ants

definitions: An organism, especially an insect, that lives in close association with or shares a nest with a species of ant.
examples: Ants are integral components of terrestrial ecosystems, owing to their manifold interactions with other organisms. They serve as seed dispersers for plants (myrmecochory), mutualists with sap-sucking insects (trophobionts), and hosts for a wide range of associate organisms (myrmecophiles) and act as both predator and prey.

definitions: (biology) Adapted to thrive in the presence of ants.
A plant that lives in association with a colony of ants. The degree of association varies and is assumed to be symbiotic in some cases.
Of or relating to myrmecophytes.

examples: He cared no more for the Devil and all his myrmidons, for he kenned brawly he was damned.

definitions: A red-brown resinous material, the dried sap of a tree of the genus Commiphora, especially Commiphora myrrha, used as perfume, incense or medicine.
(Scotland) The herb chervil.
examples: A compound of eggs boiled with myrrh, pepper, and cinnamon, taken on several successive days, is recommended by Arabs for strengthening amorous vigor.

definitions: Characteristic of the myrtle family (Myrtaceae) of plants.

A dark green shade that resembles the color of Myrtus leaves.
Cyrilla species.

definitions: (zoology) A set of growth stages in the larvae of Decapoda after protozoea and prior to the juvenile stage, in which pereiopods are used to move.
A sexual dependency on something soiled or filthy, usually a kind of undergarment after use.
examples: These paraphilias include coprophilia (preoccupation with feces), urophilia (preoccupation with urine), mysophilia (preoccupation with unclean surroundings)…

Mysophilia, for example, can probably only be an erotic turn-on when it is possible for people to be clean, which is something that has been possible only in comparatively recent times.

definitions: An abnormal fear of uncleanliness, dirt, or contamination.
examples: Presenting 14 unnerving segments, featured phobias include caetophobia (fear of hairs), henophobia (fear of young virgin girls), coprophobia (fear of feces), mysophobia (fear of contamination and germs), mazeophobia (fear of being lost), astrophobia (fear of celestial objects), mageirocophobia (fear of cooking) and oneirophobia (fear of dreams).

definitions: Of, pertaining to, or suffering from mysophobia
Difficult to understand.
Having unknown qualities.
Of unknown origin.
Deliberately evasive or enigmatic.
examples: Our boss is a mysterious man who hardly ever meets with us.
He received a mysterious phone call this morning.
Stop being mysterious and just tell me what you want.
Why he left without saying goodbye is quite mysterious.

definitions: In a mysterious manner.
examples: I put my finger to the side of my nose and nodded mysteriously.

'Well, there are some left to be sure,' answered the peasant; 'on the Sukkestad-moor there is an old jack-hare, which many a hound and sportsman have been after, but he is not so easily killed, it appears.' And the worthy peasant shook his head somewhat mysteriously.

definitions: An account, story, book, film, or play, often with the theme of crime or murder, with a surprise ending that explains all the strange events that have occurred.

A mystery play.
Something secret or unexplainable; an unknown.

examples: The truth behind the events remains a mystery.

the Mysteries of Mithras

definitions: Someone who practices mysticism.

Of, or relating to mystics, mysticism or occult mysteries; mystical.
examples: Taught he not thee—the man of eld, / Whose eyes within his eyes beheld / Heaven's numerous hierarchy span / The mystic gulf from God to man?

definitions: Relating to mystics or mysticism.
Inspiring a sense of spiritual mystery, awe, and fascination.
Having a spiritual or magical significance that transcends human understanding.
examples: Becoming more aware of the progress that scientists have made on behavioral fronts can reduce the risk that other natural scientists will resort to mystical agential accounts when they exceed the limits of their own disciplinary training.

definitions: In a mystical manner.
examples: I spoke to her of power and pride, But mystically —in such guise That she might deem it nought beside The moment’s converse; […]

definitions: A doctrine of direct communication or spiritual intuition of divine truth.

The beliefs, ideas, or thoughts of mystics.
(religion, philosophy) A transcendental union of soul or mind with the divine reality or divinity.
examples: In the history of Christian mysticism the problem how to discriminate between such messages and experiences as were really divine miracles, and such others as the demon in his malice was able to counterfeit, thus making the religious person twofold more the child of hell he was before, has always been a difficult one to solve, needing all the sagacity and experience of the best directors of conscience.

definitions: A mystifying thing.
The act of mystifying or the condition of being mystified.
examples: For a structuralist like Edmund Leach, the structure is the meaning. Genesis, for example, is about incest taboos; all the rest is noise and mystification.

By this time the calling-on arm is pulled off, and a few minutes later in comes the train, unannounced by the loud-speakers, to the mystification of many travellers.

definitions: simple past and past participle of mystify
puzzled or confused
examples: 'Do I happen to have it for disposal?' repeated Mr Carlyle in a slightly mystified tone. 'I thought you would have read that the coin has been stolen. […] '

definitions: A person who obscures or muddles information, making something appear to be a mystery.
A situation that is puzzling, or mystifying
To thoroughly confuse, befuddle, or bewilder.

Very hard to understand; baffling.
An aura of heightened interest, meaning or mystery surrounding a person or thing.

The mystique spelled out a choice—love, home, children, or other goals and purposes in life. […] The baby boom of the immediate postwar years took place in every country. But it was not permeated, in most other countries, with the mystique of feminine fulfillment.

definitions: A person or thing held in excessive or quasi-religious awe or admiration based on popular legend
An invented story, theory, or concept.

A traditional story which embodies a belief regarding some fact or phenomenon of experience, and in which often the forces of nature and of the soul are personified; a sacred narrative regarding a god, a hero, the origin of the world or of a people, etc.
Such stories as a genre.
A person or thing existing only in imagination, or whose actual existence is not verifiable.
A commonly-held but false belief, a common misconception; a fictitious or imaginary person or thing; a popular conception about a real person or event which exaggerates or idealizes reality.
examples: As for Mrs. Primmins's bones, they had been myths these twenty years.
His story is a pure myth.

definitions: (colloquial) Amazing, epic, legendary.

examples: Bellerophon attempts to become a mythic hero by perfectly imitating the actuarial program for mythic heroes.

definitions: (by extension) Not real; false or fabricated.

examples: The city was compared to a mythical paradise.

mythical creature
mythical figure

definitions: To interpret in terms of mythology.
To make into a myth.

definitions: Non- Oxford British English standard spelling of mythologize.
A person who studies mythology
The act or process of mythologizing.
examples: But that doesn’t mean that the series is bad, just peculiar, a solemn mythologization — and mystification — of surfing as unearthly pleasure and life-sapping addiction.

definitions: To construct a myth or mythology. [from 17th c.]
To make (something or someone) into a myth; to create a legend about. [from 19th c.]
examples: Most of Æsopes fables have divers senses, and severall interpretations: Those which Mythologize them, chuse some kinde of colour well-suting with the fable; but for the most part, it is no more than the first and superficiall glosse […].

definitions: The set of misconceptions and false attitudes that are held about something and contribute to its perception.
The systematic collection and study of myths.
A similar body of myths concerning an event, person or institution.
The collection of myths of a people, concerning the origin of the people, history, deities, ancestors and heroes.

definitions: (sensu lato) Any bivalve mollusc of the order Mytilida; the mussels.
